attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of increasingly aggressive behaviour. He was admitted to the brain injury rehabilitation ward five weeks ago following evacuation of a traumatic subdural haematoma. He had shown a pleasant demeanour until two days ago. He has no respiratory or urinary symptoms. He has a medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus. His only regular medication is metformin.

On examination, he appears agitated. His pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 134/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.0°C. His Glasgow Coma Scale score is 15 with impaired attention and worsening cognition. There is generalised hyperreflexia and hypertonia. Urine culture is negative. Complete blood examination and chest X-ray have been arranged.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for his current behaviour?
options: Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia
attributes.explanation:
The most likely cause of this patient's aggression is a subdural haematoma re-bleed. It is crucial to exclude organic causes of delirium in an older patient with acute behavioural changes. Common causes of delirium include infection, metabolic disturbances, and in this case, recurrent traumatic injury. The diagnosis of subdural haematoma re-bleed is supported by the patient's recent history of subdural haematoma, acute onset of behavioural changes, and upper motor neuron signs (hypertonia and hyperreflexia) on neurological examination.

Frontotemporal dementia typically does not present this acutely. Patients with frontotemporal dementia are commonly diagnosed between 40-60 years of age. The clinical presentation of frontotemporal dementia includes progressive behavioural changes with possible muscle weakness. If frontotemporal dementia is suspected, further history (including collateral history from family members) and brain MRI would be required to confirm the diagnosis. However, organic causes of delirium should be excluded first.

Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD) is an unlikely diagnosis in a patient with no known dementia diagnosis. Furthermore, acute behavioural change with impaired attention points to delirium rather than dementia.

Post-traumatic amnesia usually occurs immediately after a patient regains consciousness from a traumatic injury. It is an unlikely diagnosis in this case as the patient was stable for five weeks post-injury before developing these new symptoms.

Transient global amnesia typically presents as acute, temporary anterograde amnesia. It is self-limiting, with symptoms often resolving within 24 hours. During an episode of transient global amnesia, patients may experience disorientation regarding their location and people; however, it is unlikely to present with acute aggression as seen in this case.

In this case, the acute onset of behavioural changes, impaired attention, and upper motor neuron signs in a patient with recent head trauma strongly suggest a subdural haematoma re-bleed. Urgent neuroimaging would be indicated to confirm this diagnosis and guide further management.
learningPoints: Exclude organic causes of delirium in an elderly patient with acute behavioural changes.
Subdural haematoma re-bleed should be considered in patients with recent head trauma who develop acute neurological changes.
Upper motor neuron signs such as hyperreflexia and hypertonia can indicate intracranial pathology.
references: Marshman LA, Jakabek D, Hennessy M, Quirk F, Guazzo EP. Post-traumatic amnesia. Journal of clinical neuroscience. 2013 Nov 1;20(11):1475-81. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23791248/

Lavrador J, Teixeira J, Oliveira E, Simão D, Santos M, Simas N. Acute subdural hematoma evacuation: predictive factors of outcome. Asian Journal of Neurosurgery. 2018 Sep;13(03):565-71. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30283506/
Arena JE, Rabinstein AA. Transient global amnesia. InMayo clinic proceedings 2015;90(2):264-272. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25659242/
Frontotemporal Dementia. In: Alzheimer's Association Organisation [online]. 2022. Available at: https://www.alz.org/alzheimers-dementia/what-is-dementia/types-of-dementia/frontotemporal-dementia
